figures.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison with state-of-the-art time series baselines.
Table 2: Ablation study results of AeroSense. w/o denotes the removal of a specific module.

Figure 1: Paradigm comparison for air traffic flow prediction. The proposed situation-aware paradigmdirectlymodels airspace situation for flow prediction using only instantaneous states at time 𝑡 , without requiring a look-back window hyperparameter.

Figure 4: Temporal distribution of prediction error (MAE) over a 24-hour cycle under dayparting evaluation. AeroSense maintains lower errors than time-series baselines across all time intervals, with particularly strong robustness during peak traffic periods.
Figure 2: Overview of the AeroSense architecture. This figure illustrates the key variables and computational steps of the inference pipeline, corresponding to Equations 10–26.

Figure 5: Visualization of Situation-Aware Representation. Spatial attention weights for the same traffic situtaion at time 𝑡 under (a) AR and (b) AP flow prediction tasks (darker colors indicate higher importance). The aircraft marked by Arrow 2 receives high attention for AR prediction but is suppressed for AP prediction, reflecting task-specific adaptation. In contrast, the aircraft marked by Arrow 1 remains consistently down-weighted in both cases, indicating effective noise suppression. These patterns demonstrate that AeroSense dynamically decouples traffic relevance based on task-dependent kinematic constraints.
